Most CAM companies grow by accident.

A referral here. A board member who knew somebody. A renewal that almost didn't happen.

When growth is accidental, it isn't a strategy - it's luck. And luck doesn't scale.

Engineered growth looks different:

→ ATTRACT. A digital presence that brings boards to you.
→ CLOSE. A proposal system built to win competitive bids.
→ KEEP. A retention infrastructure that survives manager transitions.

Three engines. One system. Built for CAM.

I've sat on both sides of the management company table.

As an operator, I watched proposals come in stacked six deep - every one claiming "experienced, responsive, local." Boards stopped reading by proposal three.

What boards are actually evaluating:

→ Will this team make us look smart to homeowners?
→ Do they understand our specific community?
→ When something goes sideways, will someone pick up?
→ Are they teaching us, or just selling us?

If you're heading into RFP season, your differentiator isn't your tech stack. It's whether boards feel understood on page one.

- Skyler

5 questions every CAM company should ask before the next bid:

1. Does page one tell the board something they don't already know about themselves?
2. Are we leading with outcomes - or our org chart?
3. Have we done discovery, or are we guessing?
4. Will follow-up keep us in the conversation after the presentation?
5. If a competitor read this, would they know how to beat us?

A name and a phone number isn't a sales asset. It's homework.

Most CAM companies call leads "qualified" the moment a contact form is filled. Then they wonder why their close rate is 8%.

A qualified opportunity has context:
→ The community's actual frustration with their current manager
→ Renewal date and decision timeline
→ Board members who'll champion the conversation
→ The outcome they're trying to engineer

That's the difference between Groundwork and a lead list. We hand off opportunities our clients close at 40-60%.

Most CAM companies lose associations after manager transitions. Not because of bad service. Because there's no onboarding system.

When a manager leaves, three contracts walk with them - because the relationship lived in one head.

BoardRetain™ is the infrastructure that fixes this:

→ Knowledge transfer protocols built into every onboarding
→ Board-level relationship mapping (not just manager-level)
→ Transition playbooks that protect every association

Retention is a system. Build it on purpose.

CAI national is coming up.

What growth conversations should management companies be having before they walk in the door?

→ Where is our pipeline coming from - and is it sustainable?
→ How are we losing competitive bids? What's the pattern?
→ Which of our managers carry too much relationship risk?
→ Where will we be in 12 months if nothing changes?

CAI is the highest-density CAM conversation of the year. Walk in with sharper questions.

Why board education is a growth strategy - not just retention.

When you teach a board to be better at being a board, three things happen:

1. They renew. Educated boards understand the value of a great manager.
2. They refer. They become advocates in their networks.
3. They scale. Educated boards make better decisions, which lowers your service costs.

Most CAM companies treat board education as a checkbox. The ones who treat it as a growth lever are pulling away from everyone else.

Most CAM companies post on social hoping it generates leads.

It won't - unless your digital foundation is built to convert.

Things that have to come first:

→ A site that ranks for the searches boards actually run
→ Case studies on the page where the form lives
→ A proposal that closes what the form generates

Social amplifies. It doesn't replace.

40-60% target conversion on Groundwork-sourced opportunities.

Most lead gen sells you volume. We sell you outcomes.

The difference is qualification:

→ Every Groundwork opportunity comes with the community's actual context
→ The renewal date, the board dynamic, the unmet need
→ Pre-qualified to fit your service model - not just any HOA with a pulse

Quality > volume, every time.

#CAM #HOAManagement #Groundwork

RFP season is 6 weeks out. Most CAM companies aren't ready.

A 3-point diagnostic to run on your proposal before it goes out this fall:

1. Page one - does it speak to the board's specific situation, or to anyone with units?
2. Discovery - do you have the context to write a proposal, or are you guessing?
3. Follow-up - what happens after the presentation? Be specific.

If any of those feel shaky, you have time to fix it. Not much, but enough.
